speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 9, 1995. The survivor being interviewed is Dov Feit. The interviewer is Benjamin Weiner. We're in Brooklyn, New York, in the United States of America. And we'll be speaking in English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 9, 1995. My name is Benjamin Weiner. This is Dov Feit, the survivor being interviewed. We're in Brooklyn, New York, in the United States of America. And we'll be speaking in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please say your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Dov Feit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Feit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland, in the town, Pruchnik.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is that spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-R-U-C-H-N-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me a little bit about the history of your name-- that it hasn't always been Dov.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My name was Berr. And when I arrive in Israel, in 1942, beginning of 1942, I changed it automatically to Dov, 'cause I never liked the spelling of my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the name Berr?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was supposed to be named Bernard. But the guy who wrote in my name, he did not complete to write my name, which he wrote-- B-E-R-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he stopped halfway?

speaker: interviewee
text: Halfway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other nicknames that were used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my family used to call me [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did that name come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just a Polish nickname for love-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a big family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were nine children-- six brothers and three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did everyone else have nicknames as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Every-- every brother and sister had a nickname. My oldest brother name was Zev, but they used to call him [NON-ENGLISH]. My-- the next brother's name was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --[NON-ENGLISH]. His name was Isadore. And my oldest sister's name was Chana. They used to call her Chanya. My other sister name was Miriam. They used to call her [NON-ENGLISH]. And my brother's-- next, my brother's name was Joe, and they used to call him [NON-ENGLISH]. And then my other brother's was Israel. They used to call him [NON-ENGLISH]. Then I was Berr. They used to call me [NON-ENGLISH]. And my youngest brother was Elimeleeh. They used to call him [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the eighth of nine children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived-- were your parents born in Poland as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. My father was born in Bolekhiv. And my mother was born in Halinow, a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- were the-- were all these towns in the same part of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the towns? Very close-- very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the town of-- Pruchnik like? Was it a big town or was it very rural?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a small town. And there were just a few hundred Jews. And the whole population must have been about 2,000. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was in the lumber business. He used to own forest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They owned a factory, and a lot of fields, you know, and thousands of acres. Matter of fact, he was one of the wealthiest people in the area, and he used to be the main employee in the area. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he was also the president of the Jewish community in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was a Jewish community of a couple hundred people?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 600 Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 600 Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say the majority was religious. We used to have the main shul and two smaller shuls-- which naturally, we were there 1989, nothing was left from the shuls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the main shul look like when it was standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main shul was a very large shul, I would say, between 25, 30 foot ceiling-- beautiful shul. And I would say there-- they had a beautiful-- you know-- decorated and-- and I naturally, I used to go to shul every Saturday, and including-- I used to go during the week, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you go by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with my wife, with my sister, my nephew, and my cousin, and her brother. But in Poland, we were not all together. Like, me, my wife, and my sister, and my nephew, we traveled together. And they travel, you know, a different direction. But we met from time to time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were growing up, your father was in the lumber business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did he produce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lumber boards. He was building homes. He was supplying the logs for the railroad. In any kind of lumber that you could think about it, he was producing. And he was bringing in the trees, the logs, you know, from his own forest-- and quite a large business. And he also put his two children, the two daughters, in the lumber business in different towns, after they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of house did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have-- we had the largest house in-- in town, which-- it was a four story house. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I would say the size of the living room must have been 40 by 40, and the other rooms were huge. And we all lived in that house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share a bedroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I used to sleep in the big living room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And each of us had-- had rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a pretty big house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very big house. I-- I didn't like that big room for myself. But I slept there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like being the-- one of the youngest children in a-- in a family so big, having so many older brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't like it always, because the youngest used to be the favorite. And next to the youngest, had to fight for his rights--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --which I did. Like my parents used to go to a big town, and they said they have no-- a room for me. What I used to do before they went in on the horse and wagon, I used to hide and cover myself with hay. And they didn't see me. But when they arrived to the big town, they noticed me. But I got a punishment. [LAUGHS] But I didn't care, as long as I came to the big town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the big town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jaroslaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jaroslaw. That was the-- the biggest town in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were a lot of bigger-- big towns. But Jaroslaw was the town that my two sisters lived in. And they have, you know, lumber yards there. And we used to visit them. And also been-- the main shopping, they used to go to a big town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go to school in Pruchnik?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to public school, which was very liked by me. And as a matter of fact, 1989, when I arrived to-- in Pruchnik, this was my first stop to visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: The public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had good memories-- a lot of friends, teachers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They used to favor me because of my parents, you know? I used to be very popular, and I had a good time. What did you like to study? What were your favorite subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think math. I used to be very handy. There used to be a subject to make things. And I used to like to make things out of metal sheets. Actually, I was very good at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things would you make out of the sheets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lamps, figures, animals. And I liked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of games would you play with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to play soccer, volleyball, which I was pretty good at it. And we used to play a game which was similar to baseball. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But instead of ball, we just have a piece of wood. I used to throw the piece of wood. They used to hit it. But it was very dangerous. A lot of people got hurt. But it was a very popular game, and I liked it. But I never played it, you know, after I left Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other activities did you have while you were going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going to school in the morning till about 2:00, 3 o'clock. And right after school, we had to go to cheder, which I learned-- I didn't learn Hebrew, but I learned Chumash, you know-- Gemara-- and every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the big differences between public school and cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To cheder, I was forced to go. But in the public school, I went voluntarily. I-- they used to pull my ears if I didn't-- if I didn't concentrate, or I didn't answer the right question, or I missed a day. But I did go, 'cause I had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it important to your parents that you study Chumash and Gemara?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. My-- my father was very orthodox. And to an extent, he wore a shtreimel, and my mother wore-- wore a sheitel. And he-- like I said, he was the president of the town. And he was-- kinda important that he should give a Jewish education to his children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of-- what were his responsibilities as president of the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main responsibility for him was to keep the community united as Jews and also to represent the Jews to the Polish community. And also he, and my mother, especially, were very concerned about the livelihood of the Jewish people-- their-- he knew people who did not have food, and he made sure that they would have food every day, especially every Shabbas, you know? And my father and mother were providing them. Providing-- as a matter of fact, I used to be a part of it, to bring it over to different families.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made a package, and I walked to the people, to the families, and gave them, you know, the package.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many poor Jews in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were quite a bit. And they were not starving. But also, there were quite a bit of poor peoples who were traveling, you know, from town to town. And those people knew that they usually, like Friday night and Saturday, they used to come to shul. And they were sitting in a certain area. And many people, including my father, used to pick up people and bring them over to the house and be a part of meal; and other things he used to give them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Shabbat meal like at your home on Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat meal was the meal of the week, which was a very, very full meal-- it-- seven, eight course of dishes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gefilte fish, soups, different kind of meat, cakes, oh, side dishes-- a very wide selection of food. We used to have our own bakery in the house. And we also had-- we a lot of servants, which lived in there, you know, in the same house, which we had a-- like a bottom floor. And at the time, we didn't have our own car. The car was not as popular. We had the horse and wagons, and we had people who took care of it. Wherever we had to go, we entered in horse and wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have big Shabbat meals with eight courses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight courses-- big meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would there be singing at the table?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of singing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A lot of singing, and it was-- you really felt it-- Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father like to sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And that's how the family kept together. They were busy a whole week in the business. And Friday, Saturday, naturally, was celebrated as, really, meals, and friendship, and seeing friends, and coming over and going over. And we were in an area with a lot of forests. And normally on Shabbat, we had friends, a girlfriend. We used to meet together, and we used to go play in the playgrounds, and go in the forests. And it was very nice. We had a nice life in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would the family come together on any other holidays, too, like Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not remember if there was any holidays that the family was not together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even the sisters in the other towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They would come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always come together, the whole family. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was a nice life-- very comfortable-- but whatever had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were also telling me that you were involved with Betar, the Zionist organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my older brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your older brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: --next to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were too young.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I used to watch him, hoping one day I'm going to be involved in joining him. Because I was younger, they didn't accept me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you interested in Zionism?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't think about it at the time. For me, Betar, at the time-- I didn't think about connecting it about Israel. But an organization, which they were wearing, you know, military clothes, which was kind exciting-- and watching them. And I also used to play with friends, a war. My father used to have a big lumber yard. There used to be stacks of lumber. And we used to go into the lumber yard. We were about 30, 40, you know, kids. And actually, I was the-- the commander, the general. And it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship like in your town between the-- the Jewish community and the non-Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. The whole family was respected from the gentile. We had a lot of non-gentile friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and when I visited in 1989, in-- not sure-- they don't-- didn't recognize me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was walking the streets and approaching older people. And when I kept asking them, do you remember the Feit family? They kinda got excited. What? Are they still alive? And the next question was, when are they coming back? They were very liked. So your family occupied a position of respect in the community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both among the Jews and among the non-Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Matter of fact, my father was running also to be a senator in the Polish, you know, government. He had all the Polish people there, rich people, and which were very friendly-- very close to my father. If anything had to be decided in the town, they never did without my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things change? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Things started to change actually, in 1938, when the rumors were about Germany. You know, they was thinking to-- to go into a war with Europe. The Polish people in town became anti-Semitic. To an extent it-- there were times there was a pogrom. They were just running in the streets and hitting Jews, breaking windows. And I can never forget that they were approaching our house, and my four older brothers came out the street when they were near our house. They walked over to them and said, don't you dare do any damages. Don't you try. Because we're gonna fight you. And they just kept going. They didn't do anything. But it was a very tense situation. You were-- you were afraid to get out from the house in the evenings, at night. You were afraid to walk by yourself. It came to-- to a situation that my parents were very afraid. And we were not free to walk around, you know, in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were 12 years old--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It seems like things changed very suddenly and very drastically.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was when the war broke out. We knew that the Polish army is putting up the resistance. The German-- the Germans were bombarding. They never bombard our city But in the beginning, they took me to my older sister, and I stayed there for a few days. And when I saw planes bombarding the city, for me it was a happiness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Look at this. Planes are throwing things and bombarding. What's going on? You know, homes are-- big buildings are falling down. And I ran to the center of the town by myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody realized what happening. It was a sudden, you know, bombarding day. And I run to the center of the city. But they were-- they were digging bunkers. And I remember I took a shovel and I helped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Naturally, then I went back, you know. And my parents and my other two sisters joined the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the war began, they returned to Pruchnik?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Then when the war began, we all got together. We lived-- we all lived in-- in the house, you know, in Pruchnik. And naturally, we were hearing, you know, rumors about the German are advancing. And we also heard that a town not far from us, they came in and they killed 30-- dirty, you know, 30 Jews. Another town, they killed Jews. But we-- you know, my family never believed they would come to our town. Then my-- my father decided that the older brothers will try to leave town, and-- for a few days, till the situation will change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, they were traveling in-- from one place to another. And finally, my father stopped and said, what are we doing? Where are we going? The whole family is home. We don't know where we going. And they decided to go back.
